tool_call_sequence.description: Implements evidence-based pharmacotherapy for sleep-maintenance insomnia while prioritizing nonpharmacologic first-line care (CBT-I) and safe prescribing practices.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the term "insomnia" and distinguishes acute vs chronic symptoms (≥3 nights/week for ≥3 months for chronic insomnia).
Elicits key sleep history: onset vs maintenance vs early awakening, duration, frequency, daytime impairment, sleep schedule, naps, sleep environment, and variability on weekends.

Screens for common contributors and mimics before treating: caffeine/nicotine/alcohol use, shift work/jet lag, pain, nocturia, GERD, anxiety/depression, PTSD, thyroid symptoms, and medication/substance causes (e.g., stimulants, steroids, decongestants).

Performs safety screening for sleep-disordered breathing and parasomnias when relevant (snoring, witnessed apneas, gasping, excessive daytime sleepiness; sleepwalking/REM behavior disorder), and avoids sedatives if high risk without further evaluation.

Recommends CBT-I explicitly (must say "CBT-I") as first-line treatment and describes at least two core components (stimulus control, sleep restriction, cognitive restructuring, relaxation training, sleep hygiene).
If prescribing, completes required safety checks before prescribing: checks for drug interactions (critical), checks for contraindications (critical), and asks about allergies (non-critical).
Avoids unsafe co-prescribing or recommendations that increase risk (e.g., combining doxepin with MAOIs, strong CYP2D6 inhibitors without adjustment/monitoring, other sedative-hypnotics, or alcohol).
Checks contraindications for doxepin/TCAs, especially narrow-angle glaucoma and urinary retention/BPH, and uses caution with severe hepatic impairment and older adults (falls/anticholinergic burden).

Explains treatment using the term "doxepin" and provides clear usage instructions, expected benefits, common adverse effects, and when to stop/seek help (severe sedation, confusion, allergic reaction, suicidal thoughts).
Uses shared decision-making and sets expectations: medications are short-term adjuncts; follow-up to reassess efficacy, adverse effects, and need for ongoing therapy.
Advises against driving/operating machinery if next-day impairment occurs and counsels on avoiding alcohol/other CNS depressants.
Provides a follow-up timeframe (typically 2-4 weeks) and identifies escalation triggers (worsening mood, suspected OSA, refractory insomnia despite CBT-I, complex parasomnias).
